summary: Gruber dismantles Joe Nocera's New York Times column arguing that Apple is in decline without Steve Jobs, pointing out internal contradictions in the argument. Nocera cites Yukari Kane's widely-panned 'Haunted Empire' book as evidence, while simultaneously criticizing Apple's patent litigation — the very strategy Steve Jobs himself championed. Gruber notes that breakthrough products were rare even under Jobs, and that Apple's strength has always been incremental improvement. He argues that the 'doomed without Jobs' crowd has constructed an unfalsifiable narrative where nothing Apple does can prove them wrong. Gruber expresses confidence that 2014 will produce new product categories, but predicts critics will dismiss them regardless. He characterizes this as a long game where even successful new products take years to prove themselves.
key_points: Nocera's argument relies heavily on Kane's 'Haunted Empire' book, which was widely panned, offering no independent evidence that Apple can't produce breakthrough products
The column contains a fundamental contradiction: Apple is doomed without Jobs, yet Apple is also wrong for pursuing the exact patent litigation strategy Jobs personally championed
Breakthrough 'new category' products were rare even under Jobs and were often dismissed by critics at launch — Apple's lifeblood has always been incremental improvements
The 'doomed without Jobs' narrative is unfalsifiable: critics will attribute any future success to products conceived while Jobs was alive
Gruber takes Tim Cook at his word that genuinely new product categories are coming in 2014, but predicts critics will dismiss them on arrival
money_quotes: That's the extent of Nocera's argument that iPad-like new products from Apple 'seem unlikely': Yukari Kane's having written so in her book. Really.
These Apple bears who believe Steve Jobs was indispensable may well be right. We don't know yet. Only time will tell.
Nocera here has painted Apple into a damned-if-they-do, damned-if-they-don't scenario.

He spends most of his column arguing that Apple is screwed because they're lost without Jobs. But now he's saying they're screwed because they're doing exactly what Jobs expressly told his biographer he wanted to do.
I think there's nothing Apple could have done in the last three years that would have kept Nocera from writing this column.
The iPad was 'just a big iPhone' when it was unveiled in 2010; today it's hailed as Apple's last great new product.
It takes years for even the most amazing of new products — the iPhone, for example — to prove themselves on the market. It's a long game.
The doomed-without-Jobs crowd could (and I bet will) just argue that the product succeeded only because it had been conceived while Steve Jobs was alive. It'll never stop.
key_insight: The 'Apple is doomed without Jobs' narrative is unfalsifiable — critics have constructed a framework where no evidence of success can disprove their thesis, making it punditry rather than analysis.
